subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Piecewise f(x) = {1-2sin(x), e^(-4x)} — Continuity at x=0, Piecewise Derivative & Average Value
stimulus:
Let $f$ be the function defined by
$$f(x) = \begin{cases} 1 - 2\sin x & \text{if } x \le 0 \\ e^{-4x} & \text{if } x > 0 \end{cases}$$

rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Shows both one-sided limits equal 1: $\lim_{x \to 0^-} f(x) = 1 - 2\sin(0) = 1$ and $\lim_{x \to 0^+} f(x) = e^0 = 1$
1 pt: Correct conclusion: since $f(0) = 1 - 2\sin(0) = 1$ and $\lim_{x \to 0} f(x) = 1 = f(0)$, $f$ is continuous at $x = 0$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $\lim_{x \to 0^-}(1-2\sin x) = 1$, $\lim_{x \to 0^+} e^{-4x} = 1$, and $f(0) = 1$. All three equal, so $f$ is continuous at $x=0$.

parts.prompt: Find $f'(x)$ for $x < 0$ and find $f'(x)$ for $x > 0$. Determine whether $f$ has a local minimum, local maximum, or neither at each of the $x$-values for which $f'(x) = -3$.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct piecewise derivative: $f'(x) = \begin{cases} -2\cos x & x < 0 \\ -4e^{-4x} & x > 0 \end{cases}$

1 pt: Correct solution: for $x < 0$: $-2\cos x = -3 \Rightarrow \cos x = \frac{3}{2}$, which has no solution; for $x > 0$: $-4e^{-4x} = -3 \Rightarrow e^{-4x} = \frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow x = -\frac{1}{4}\ln\frac{3}{4} = \frac{1}{4}\ln\frac{4}{3}$

1 pt: Correct conclusion: $f'(x) < -3$ for $0 < x < \frac{1}{4}\ln\frac{4}{3}$ and $f'(x) > -3$ for $x > \frac{1}{4}\ln\frac{4}{3}$; $f'$ changes from more negative to less negative (increasing through $-3$), so $f$ has neither a local min nor a local max at $x = \frac{1}{4}\ln\frac{4}{3}$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f'(x) = -3$ only at $x = \frac{1}{4}\ln\frac{4}{3}$ (in $(0,\infty)$). $f'$ does not change sign there (remains negative), so $f$ has neither a local minimum nor a local maximum.

parts.prompt: Find the average value of $f$ on the interval $-1 \le x \le 1$.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct setup: $\frac{1}{2}\int_{-1}^{1} f(x)\, dx = \frac{1}{2}\left[\int_{-1}^{0}(1 - 2\sin x)\, dx + \int_{0}^{1} e^{-4x}\, dx\right]$
1 pt: Correct antiderivative for left piece: $\left[x + 2\cos x\right]_{-1}^{0} = (0 + 2) - (-1 + 2\cos(-1)) = 3 - 2\cos(1)$
1 pt: Correct antiderivative for right piece: $\left[-\frac{1}{4}e^{-4x}\right]_{0}^{1} = -\frac{1}{4}e^{-4} + \frac{1}{4}$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Average value $= \frac{1}{2}\int_{-1}^{1} f(x)\, dx = \frac{1}{2}\left[(x+2\cos x)\big|_{-1}^{0} + \left(-\frac{1}{4}e^{-4x}\right)\big|_{0}^{1}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left(3 - 2\cos 1 + \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{4e^4}\right) = \frac{13}{8} - \cos 1 - \frac{1}{8e^4}$.
